title: How the body stores the impact of being yelled at as a child
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The voice that stays in your bones 

 I was in my thirties before I realized that my shoulders didn't have to live at my ears. That my jaw could unclench....
htmlContent:
The voice that stays in your bones

I was in my thirties before I realized that my shoulders didn't have to live at my ears. That my jaw could unclench. That a sudden loud voice didn't mean danger was coming. It sounds obvious when I write it out like that. But for years, I walked around in a body that was bracing for impact. Waiting for the shout that never came. And then one day, a friend slammed a cabinet door a little too hard and I was gone. Not physically. But inside. My heart was racing. My throat was tight. I was eight years old again, frozen in the kitchen, being yelled at for something I couldn't even remember.

Here's the thing. That reaction wasn't me being dramatic. It wasn't me being weak. It was my body remembering. And it was trying to protect me.

Being yelled at as a child doesn't just hurt in the moment. It gets stored. In your muscles. In your nervous system. In the way you flinch at sudden movements. In the way you apologize for things that aren't your fault. In the way you scan every room for signs of danger, even when you're safe. The body keeps the score. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote that famous line and spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body, would tell you that the impact of chronic verbal aggression is real. It's measurable. It changes your brain and your biology.

And nobody told us that. So we thought we were just broken.

We're not broken. We're just carrying something we were never meant to carry.

Let me show you how it happens. And more , what you can actually do about it.

The nervous system learns to expect the worst

Your nervous system has one job. Keep you alive. It doesn't care about your feelings. It doesn't care about logic. It cares about threat detection. And when you're a child, your nervous system is still being built. It's learning what's safe and what's dangerous based on what happens to you. If you're yelled at regularly, your system learns that loud voices, angry faces, and sudden shifts in tone mean danger. It doesn't matter if the yelling is "just words." Your body doesn't distinguish between a verbal threat and a physical one. To your nervous system, a threat is a threat.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, explains that our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. He calls this process neuroception. It happens below conscious awareness. So when someone raises their voice at you today, your body might respond as if you're still that child. Your heart rate goes up. Your breathing gets shallow. Your muscles tense. You might freeze. You might want to run. You might get defensive and snap back. These are all survival responses. They were adaptive when you were small and helpless. But now, they can feel like they're running your life.

Stay with me here. This isn't about blaming your parents or wallowing in victimhood. This is about understanding why certain things feel so hard. Why you can't just "let it go" no matter how much you try. Because the body doesn't let go of what it learned in order to survive.

One of the most common ways this shows up is in hypervigilance. You're always scanning. For the subtle shift in someone's tone. For the slight change in their face. For the moment when everything tips from okay to not okay. You're exhausted because your nervous system is working overtime, all the time. And you might not even realize you're doing it. Right? Because it's just been your normal for so long.

Another way it shows up is in the body itself. Chronic tension in the shoulders, neck, and jaw. Digestive issues. Headaches. Fatigue. The body holds the memory of bracing for the yell. And it keeps bracing, long after the yelling has stopped. Think about it. Your body is still preparing for a threat that happened years ago. That's a lot of energy spent on a ghost.

The freeze response that becomes a way of life

When a child is yelled at repeatedly, they often learn that fighting back or running away doesn't work. So they freeze. They go still. They make themselves small. They try to disappear. This is a valid survival strategy. It works in the moment. The problem is that the freeze response can become a default setting. You learn to shut down when conflict arises. You learn to dissociate when things get intense. You learn to numb out rather than feel the fear, the shame, the anger that's sitting right under the surface.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in sensorimotor psychotherapy, talks about how trauma is stored in the body through incomplete action. When you froze, you didn't complete the fight or flight response. The energy got stuck. And it stays stuck until you can release it. That's why you might feel inexplicably angry or sad or terrified in situations that don't seem to warrant it. The body is trying to finish what it started.

I remember a client telling me that she would literally go mute when her boss raised his voice. Not because she was being defiant. But because her throat would close. She couldn't get words out. That's a freeze response. Her body remembered that speaking up when someone was angry was dangerous. So it shut her down to protect her. Wild, right? The body doesn't know that the boss isn't her father. It just knows that a loud voice equals threat, and threat equals freeze.

This is where the shame gets layered on. Because you might tell yourself, "Why can't I just speak up? Why am I so weak? Why can't I handle a simple disagreement?" And that self-blame just adds another layer of stress to an already overwhelmed system. You're not weak. You're responding to programming that was installed before you had a choice.

How the body literally reshapes itself

Here's what I wish someone had told me twenty years ago. The brain is plastic. It can change. And so can the body. The patterns that were wired in through years of being yelled at can be rewired. It takes time. It takes practice. But it's possible.

Dan Siegel, the clinical professor of psychiatry who studies interpersonal neurobiology, uses the phrase "what fires together, wires together." Your nervous system has a well-worn path from loud voice to fear response. Every time that path gets activated, it gets a little deeper. But you can create new paths. You can teach your system that a loud voice doesn't always mean danger. That you're not that child anymore. That you have resources now that you didn't have then.

One of the most effective ways to start this process is through regulation. Learning to calm your nervous system from the bottom up. Not by telling yourself to relax. But by giving your body the signals it needs to feel safe. Slow, deep breathing. Orienting to your environment. Noticing your feet on the floor. Gentle movement. These aren't just nice ideas. They're actual tools for changing your nervous system's response.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate, the author, explains how chronic stress, including the stress of being raised in a verbally aggressive environment, can literally make you sick. It's a hard read. But it's a necessary one if you want to understand the full picture of what's happening in your body.

Another resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. She walks you through exercises you can actually do to shift your nervous system out of survival mode. It's not just theory. It's usable.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. It's a workbook style book that gives you concrete steps to build safety in your body. I recommend it to almost everyone I talk to.

The long road back to your body

I'm not going to tell you that this is easy. It's not. There will be days when you feel like you're making progress and days when you feel like you're right back at square one. That's normal. That's how healing works. It's not a straight line. It's more like a spiral. You come back to the same issues, but from a slightly different angle each time.

One thing that helped me was a weighted blanket. It provides gentle pressure that can signal safety to the nervous system. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). It's not a cure. But it can help you feel more grounded, especially at night when the body tends to hold more tension.

The goal isn't to get rid of the history. You can't. The goal is to change your relationship to it. To stop being run by it. To have more choice in how you respond. To let your body know, over and over again, that it's safe now. That the yelling is over. That you can come out of hiding.

And here's the hardest part. You might have to grieve. Grieve the childhood you didn't get. Grieve the sense of safety you never had. Grieve the fact that your body learned to protect itself from people who were supposed to protect you. That grief is real. And it deserves to be felt.

But so does the hope. The hope that comes from knowing that your body is not your enemy. It was never your enemy. It was trying to keep you alive. And now, it can learn that you're alive. That you made it. That you can rest.

Sit with that. Let it land. You made it. You're still here. And your body can learn to trust that.

Healing Journey Resources
